final_answer: To isolate whether the latency originates in the database or the network, first instrument the request path: collect timestamps for request start, DB query start/end, and response send (N1:0:e504efc8). Enable the DB slow‑query log to capture any statements exceeding a threshold (N1:1:77a4e562) and run pg_stat_statements or similar to identify the offending queries (N2:0:3575e928). In parallel, run network latency tests (ping, traceroute) from the app server to the DB server during slow periods (N1:2:46926cba, N2:4:5bc7bf7b) and capture packets on the DB connection to detect retransmissions or loss (N1:3:8ead2c21, N3:1:d675ebde). Compare the average DB execution time against the total request time; if DB time dominates, focus on database tuning (N1:4:840d81ac), otherwise, if RTT spikes or packet loss align with slow requests, suspect network congestion (N1:5:29f1704c, N2:5:6882b2ca). Quick DB fixes include increasing the connection‑pool size or enabling connection reuse (N1:6:44da40ab) and adding missing indexes or rewriting slow queries (N1:7:2f99837b). Quick network mitigations involve restarting the NIC, verifying MTU, enabling TCP window scaling, or increasing socket buffers (N3:7:252dc72c). Correlate the timestamps from logs, packet captures, and network tests to pinpoint the dominant source and apply the appropriate quick fix while planning longer‑term optimizations.
